title: Optical detection of the electron spin resonances of G centers in silicon
authors: Félix Cache, Krithika V. R., Tobias Herzig, Andrej Yu Kuznetsov, Sébastien Pezzagna, Marco Abbarchi, Isabelle Robert-Philip, Jean-Michel Gérard, Guillaume Cassabois, Vincent Jacques, Anaïs Dréau
abstract: Color centers in silicon are emerging as promising platforms for quantum technologies. Among them, the G center has attracted considerable interest owing to its bright telecom O-band single-photon emission and its optically addressable metastable electron-spin triplet state. Here we investigate the spin properties of ensembles of G centers under above-band-gap excitation. We elucidate the spin photo-dynamics giving rise to the optical detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) response of G centers. The optimal pulsed sequence for measuring the ODMR spectrum of the G defects is identified, along with the temperature and optical-power regimes maximizing the spin readout contrast. Through magneto-optical measurements, we detect a level-anticrossing of the G center electron spin states. At last, we demonstrate coherent spin control of the defects, and characterize their spin-coherence properties. Unveiling the spin degree of freedom of the G center opens new avenues for the realization of quantum memories and quantum registers based on silicon color centers.
summary: This paper investigates the electron spin properties of G centers in silicon, demonstrating that their spin states can be optically detected and coherently controlled. By characterizing the spin-dependent lifetimes and decoherence, the study establishes G centers as promising candidates for silicon-based quantum memories and registers.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the spin-dependent photodynamics, electron spin resonance (ESR) properties, and coherent control of G centers in silicon to evaluate their potential for quantum technologies.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated successful coherent spin control via Rabi oscillations, identified optimal pulse sequences for ODMR readout, and characterized spin-coherence properties, revealing that an electron-spin bath is the primary decoherence source.
method: The study uses low-temperature confocal microscopy, time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL), and optically detected magnetic resonance (ODMR) spectroscopy with pulsed laser and microwave sequences.
model_or_system: The system consists of ensembles of G centers in silicon (Si:Sii), which are color centers with an S=1 metastable spin-triplet state and an S=0 ground state, described by a zero-field splitting Hamiltonian and a Zeeman term.
key_observables: Photoluminescence (PL) intensity, zero-phonon line (ZPL) at 1279 nm, E-line at 1380 nm, Rabi oscillations, and spin-coherence times (T2*, Techo, T_CPMG).
important_parameters: Temperature (4-24 K), magnetic field (up to 50 mT), zero-field splitting parameters (D = -1210 MHz, E = 520 MHz), and spin lifetimes (tau_0 approx 2 us, tau_plus/minus approx 40-50 us).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the primary source of dephasing in the ensemble is an electron-spin bath rather than a nuclear-spin bath, as evidenced by the lack of improvement with 28Si purification.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the G center structure, energy levels, and PL spectra; Fig 2 displays TRPL traces showing PL peaks for different delays.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the G center system, details the experimental setup and spin-dependent photodynamics, presents the ODMR spectroscopy and optimal readout parameters, demonstrates coherent spin control via Rabi oscillations, and concludes with a discussion on coherence limits and future applications in quantum memory.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in quantum optics and open quantum systems as it demonstrates the coherent manipulation of a spin-triplet system and provides detailed insights into spin-dependent relaxation and decoherence mechanisms in a solid-state platform.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Microscopic structure of the G center in silicon. (b) Energy level structure of the G center and relaxation rates. {|0⟩, |+⟩, |−⟩} are the zero-magnetic-field spin eigenstates of the metastable spin triplet level. (c) PL scan of the #S1 sample. Scale bar is 5 µm. (d) PL spectrum of the G center ensemble #A, recorded at 6 K.

FIG. 2. (a) TRPL traces acquired on ensemble #A for delays τ of 0.3, 10 and 300 µs, at B = 0 and B = 50 mT aligned along [001]. Colored areas indicate the 200-ns integration window used to infer the signal S (see pulse sequence on top). The normalization signal R is taken at the end of the previous laser pulse. (b),(c),(d) For the three G center ensembles, evolution of S/R versus waiting time τ in the dark. Solid lines are guides to the eye. Optical powers: #A 10 µW, #B 3 µW, #C 3 µW. (e) Normalized difference signal ∆SB = S/RB̸=0 −S/RB=0 for the three G ensembles. Solid line is the result of data fitting with a � e−τ/τ1 −e−τ/τeff (see text).

FIG. 3. (a) Experimental results of the differential TRPL pulse sequence with increasing temperature. Solid curves are data fitting results with a(e−τ/τ1 −e−τ/τeff ). (b) Evolution of τ1 and τ0 with temperature (see main text). (c) Maximum value of data fitting from (a) versus temperature. Solid line represents the fitting results with an Arrhenius law providing activation energy: Ea = 8.7(7) meV.

FIG. 4. (a) TRPL signal with a 1-µs laser pulse every 15 µs, measured at different optical excitation powers for B = 0 (black) and B = 50 mT along [001] crystal axis (color). (b) Simplified energy level structure showing a possi- ble photo-detrapping mechanism induced by photoionization under green laser excitation.

FIG. 5. (a) ODMR spectrum measured on the G center en- semble at B = 0 mT, using the pulsed sequence displayed on top with 1 µs laser pulses, 5 µs waiting time and 120 ns MW pulse. The ODMR contrast C is calculated by dividing the SMW signal integrated on the first 200 ns of the laser pulse, by the out-of-resonance value. Solid line is the result of data fitting with the sum of two Lorentzian functions. (b) Evolu- tion of C of the waiting times preceding (ta) and following (tb) the MW pulse. MW frequency was set to the |+⟩↔|0⟩ transition. Left: experimental data. Right: simulation per- formed using the MS lifetimes provided in Sec. IV.
